examples.title: Forward Chaining in Production System
examples.description: Implementation of forward chaining algorithm showing recognition-action cycle
examples.explanation: This Python code implements forward chaining: it starts with facts in working memory, matches rules whose conditions are satisfied, resolves conflicts by selecting the first applicable rule, and executes it to add new facts. The cycle repeats until no more rules can fire.
examples.timeComplexity: O(R × C × F) where R=rules, C=conditions, F=facts
examples.spaceComplexity: O(F + R) for storing facts and rules

examples.title: Backward Chaining Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for goal-driven backward chaining inference
examples.code:
FUNCTION Backward_Chain(goal):
    // Check if goal is already in working memory
    IF goal IN Working_Memory THEN
        RETURN True
    
    // Find rules that can conclude the goal
    rules_concluding_goal = []
    FOR each rule IN Production_Rules DO
        IF goal IN rule.conclusions THEN
            rules_concluding_goal.append(rule)
    
    // If no rule can conclude goal, return failure
    IF rules_concluding_goal IS EMPTY THEN
        RETURN False
    
    // Try each rule that can conclude the goal
    FOR each rule IN rules_concluding_goal DO
        // Try to prove all conditions of the rule
        proof_successful = True
        FOR each condition IN rule.conditions DO
            IF NOT Backward_Chain(condition) THEN
                proof_successful = False
                BREAK
        
        // If all conditions proved, goal is established
        IF proof_successful THEN
            Add goal TO Working_Memory
            RETURN True
    
    RETURN False

// Main execution
Working_Memory = { /* initial facts */ }
Goal = "diagnosis_found"
IF Backward_Chain(Goal) THEN
    Print "Goal achieved"
ELSE
    Print "Goal cannot be achieved"
examples.explanation: Backward chaining starts from a goal and recursively tries to prove each condition by finding rules that can establish those conditions. It works backwards from conclusions to premises until it either proves the goal or finds it cannot be satisfied.
examples.timeComplexity: O(B^d) where B=branching factor, d=depth of proof tree

examples.title: Conflict Resolution with Multiple Strategies
examples.description: Demonstrates three control strategies for conflict resolution
examples.code:
FUNCTION Resolve_Conflict(applicable_rules, strategy):
    
    SWITCH strategy OF
        
        CASE "lexicographic":
            // Sort by priority number (lower = higher priority)
            SORT applicable_rules BY rule.priority ASCENDING
            RETURN applicable_rules[0]
        
        CASE "specificity":
            // Rule with more conditions is more specific
            SORT applicable_rules BY number_of_conditions DESCENDING
            RETURN applicable_rules[0]
        
        CASE "recency":
            // Rule using most recently added facts wins
            FOR each rule IN applicable_rules DO
                rule.recent_score = 0
                FOR each condition IN rule.conditions DO
                    IF condition.added_time > current_time - threshold THEN
                        rule.recent_score = rule.recent_score + 1
            SORT applicable_rules BY recent_score DESCENDING
            RETURN applicable_rules[0]
        
        CASE "refraction":
            // Exclude rules that just fired
            FOR each rule IN applicable_rules DO
                IF rule.last_fired_time IS NOT NULL AND
                   Working_Memory NOT CHANGED SINCE rule.last_fired_time THEN
                    REMOVE rule FROM applicable_rules
            IF applicable_rules NOT EMPTY THEN
                RETURN applicable_rules[0]
            ELSE
                RETURN NULL
    
// Example: Using specificity ordering
rules = [
    {id: 1, conditions: [A], priority: 1},
    {id: 2, conditions: [A, B], priority: 2},
    {id: 3, conditions: [A, B, C], priority: 3}
]
selected = Resolve_Conflict(rules, "specificity")
// Selected: Rule 3 (most specific - 3 conditions)
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates three common conflict resolution strategies: Lexicographic (priority numbers), Specificity (more conditions = higher priority), and Recency (uses recent facts). The refraction strategy prevents the same rule from firing repeatedly on unchanged facts.
examples.timeComplexity: O(R log R) for sorting applicable rules
